cards.definition: The smallest, fundamental unit of ordinary matter that forms chemical elements.
A substance consisting of atoms which all have the same number of protons; neutral overall charge.
An atom or molecule with a net electric charge due to loss or gain of electrons.
A positively charged ion (loses electrons).
A negatively charged ion (gains electrons).
A compound made of metal cations and non-metal anions held together by ionic bonds.
The force that attracts oppositely charged particles (ions) and repels like charges.
Strong electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions formed by electron transfer.
An ordered, repeating three-dimensional arrangement of ions in an ionic compound.
A compound made of metal cations surrounded by a 'sea' of delocalised electrons.
Electrostatic attraction between metal cations and delocalised electrons.
Bonds where attraction extends uniformly in all directions, e.g., ionic and metallic bonds.
A compound made of molecules held together by covalent bonds and intermolecular forces.
Intramolecular bond formed by sharing pairs of valence electrons between atoms.
Weak forces of attraction between molecules, including temporary dipoles, permanent dipoles, and hydrogen bonding.
A pair of valence electrons not involved in bonding (non-bonding electrons).
Chemical principle that atoms tend to bond to have eight electrons in their valence shell for stability.
A 2D representation showing valence electrons as dots and covalent bonds as lines.
Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion theory predicting 3D molecular shapes by repulsion of electron pairs.
Organic compounds made only of carbon and hydrogen atoms.
Saturated hydrocarbon with only single bonds; general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂.
Unsaturated hydrocarbon with at least one carbon-carbon double bond; general formula CₙH₂ₙ.
Unsaturated hydrocarbon with at least one carbon-carbon triple bond; general formula CₙH₂ₙ₋₂.
Specific group of atoms responsible for characteristic reactions of a compound (e.g., -OH in alcohols).
Part of the name indicating the number of carbon atoms in the main chain (meth-, eth-, prop-, etc.).
Part of the name indicating the type of hydrocarbon or functional group (-ane, -ene, -yne, -ol).
Hydrocarbon branch attached to the main carbon chain but not part of it (e.g., methyl, ethyl).
Shows the exact number and types of atoms in a molecule (e.g., C₆H₁₄).
Graphic representation showing how atoms are bonded in a molecule.
Shorthand notation listing atoms in order bonded, grouping hydrogens next to carbons.
Simplified organic formula where carbon atoms are vertices/ends of lines and hydrogens are implied.
Compounds with the same molecular formula but different connectivity of atoms.
Ring-shaped hydrocarbons with alternating single and double bonds (e.g., benzene).
Process of breaking long-chain hydrocarbons into shorter, more useful molecules using heat and catalysts.
Separation technique that separates mixtures based on different boiling points.
Laboratory technique to heat reactions at constant temperature without loss of solvent by condensation.
Refers to the number of hydrogens attached to carbons; saturated hydrocarbons have maximum hydrogens.
Reaction where atoms or groups are added to an unsaturated molecule, breaking double or triple bonds.
Reaction where atoms or groups are removed from a molecule, forming double bonds.
In addition reactions, the hydrogen attaches to the carbon with more hydrogens already attached.
In elimination reactions, the double bond forms at the carbon with fewer hydrogens (least saturated).
Organic compounds containing carbon, hydrogen, and at least one halogen atom (F, Cl, Br, I).
Organic compounds with at least one hydroxyl (-OH) group attached to a carbon.
Organic compounds containing a carboxyl group (-COOH).
Exothermic reaction where a fuel reacts with oxygen to produce carbon dioxide, water, and energy.
Heat content of a system; change in enthalpy is heat absorbed or released during a reaction.
Reaction that releases heat; ΔH is negative.
Combustion with sufficient oxygen producing CO₂ and H₂O.
Combustion with limited oxygen producing CO, C (soot), and H₂O.
